breastMilkAlcohol.name: Breast Milk Alcohol Calculator
breastMilkAlcohol.description: Calculate when alcohol clears from breast milk after drinking.

meta.title: Breast Milk Alcohol Calculator — When Is Milk Alcohol-Free?
meta.description: Calculate when your breast milk is alcohol-free after drinking. Uses Widmark formula, body weight, and personalized clearance time. Free, instant, no sign-up.

breastMilkAlcohol.title: Breast Milk Alcohol Calculator
breastMilkAlcohol.description: Estimate the time it takes for alcohol to clear from your bloodstream — and therefore your breast milk.
breastMilkAlcohol.milkNotSafe: Milk contains alcohol — do not breastfeed
breastMilkAlcohol.guidanceText: The American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) and CDC recommend avoiding alcohol while breastfeeding. If you do drink, wait until the alcohol has cleared before nursing — pumping does NOT speed up elimination.
breastMilkAlcohol.howItWorksText: Alcohol passes freely between blood and breast milk — milk alcohol level mirrors blood alcohol level (BAC). The body eliminates alcohol at roughly 0.015% per hour. Calculation uses the Widmark formula: alcohol (g) / (body weight × 0.55).
breastMilkAlcohol.referenceTable: Rule of thumb per standard drink (≈14 g alcohol)

faq.q: Does alcohol pass into breast milk?
faq.a: Yes. Alcohol passes freely into breast milk — the concentration mirrors blood alcohol concentration (BAC). Peak milk alcohol occurs 30–60 minutes after drinking.

faq.q: Does pumping and dumping speed up alcohol clearance?
faq.a: No. Alcohol stays in the bloodstream until metabolized — fresh milk will still contain alcohol. Only time clears alcohol. Coffee, water, and exercise do not help.

faq.q: How fast does the body eliminate alcohol?
faq.a: Roughly 0.015% BAC per hour. For one standard drink (14 g alcohol), a 60 kg / 130 lb woman needs about 2 hours. Larger amounts take proportionally longer.

faq.q: Can I breastfeed after one glass of wine?
faq.a: After a 5 oz glass of wine (12%) for a 60 kg / 130 lb woman, wait roughly 2 hours. When in doubt, wait longer or feed previously pumped milk.

faq.q: Is occasional alcohol safe for the baby?
faq.a: AAP and WHO recommend avoiding alcohol while breastfeeding. Some studies show negative effects on infant sleep. Occasional moderate drinking with adequate clearance time is generally considered acceptable.
The Widmark formula is medically established but estimates can vary ±20–30% based on individual factors — stomach contents, liver function, medications, body composition.
